Day 12 - Zephyr¶

Overview¶

Day 12 - Zephyr¶

Focus¶

This day concentrates on Zephyr kernel primitives and build workflow.

Learning Objectives¶

  • Understand the core concept and where it appears in embedded products.

  • Build or reason about one practical implementation pattern.

  • Identify failure modes and prevention checks for production firmware.

Deliverables¶

  • Concept notes in lesson.rst.

  • Practice problems in exercises.rst.

  • Guided answer strategy in solutions.rst.

Definition of Done¶

  • You can explain the concept without notes.

  • You can map the concept to at least one real product scenario.

  • You can list at least three verification checks for correctness.

Lesson¶

Day 12 Lesson - Zephyr RTOS¶

Introduction¶

Zephyr is a modern, Linux Foundation-backed RTOS designed for IoT and connected devices with extensive hardware support.

Architecture¶

Key differences from FreeRTOS: - Device tree for hardware description - Kconfig for configuration - Native networking stack (TCP/IP, Bluetooth, Thread) - Rich device driver model - POSIX API support

Thread Creation¶

#include <zephyr/kernel.h>

#define STACK_SIZE 1024
#define THREAD_PRIORITY 5

K_THREAD_STACK_DEFINE(my_stack, STACK_SIZE);
struct k_thread my_thread_data;
k_tid_t my_tid;

void my_entry_point(void *p1, void *p2, void *p3)
{
    while (1) {
        printk("Thread running\n");
        k_sleep(K_MSEC(1000));
    }
}

my_tid = k_thread_create(&my_thread_data, my_stack,
                         K_THREAD_STACK_SIZEOF(my_stack),
                         my_entry_point,
                         NULL, NULL, NULL,
                         THREAD_PRIORITY, 0, K_NO_WAIT);

Synchronization Primitives¶

  • Semaphores: k_sem_*

  • Mutexes: k_mutex_*

  • Message queues: k_msgq_*

  • Mailboxes: k_mbox_*

  • Pipes: k_pipe_*

  • Work queues: k_work_*

Device Drivers¶

Zephyr’s device model provides consistent API across hardware platforms.

const struct device *uart_dev = device_get_binding("UART_0");
uart_irq_callback_set(uart_dev, uart_cb);
uart_irq_rx_enable(uart_dev);

Key Advantages¶

  1. Comprehensive hardware support

  2. Built-in networking/Bluetooth

  3. CMake-based build (west tool)

  4. Security features (TLS, secure boot)

  5. Active development community

When to Use Zephyr¶

  • IoT/connected devices

  • Bluetooth/WiFi requirements

  • Need for Linux-like development experience

  • Rapid prototyping with diverse hardware

Exercises¶

Day 12 Exercises - Zephyr RTOS¶

Exercise 1: Port existing FreeRTOS project to Zephyr.

Exercise 2: Implement BLE peripheral with Zephyr Bluetooth stack.

Exercise 3: Build IoT sensor node with MQTT over WiFi using native Zephyr networking.

Solutions¶

Day 12 Solutions - Zephyr RTOS¶

Complete Zephyr projects demonstrating device tree configuration, networking integration, and cross-platform portability.
